docs.title: Convergen
docs.text:
High-performance Go code generator for type-to-type copy functions

Convergen generates functions that copy field to field between two types, eliminating boilerplate code and reducing errors in type conversions.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
Write an annotated interface:

//go:build convergen

package sample

import (
    "time"
    "github.com/sample/myapp/domain"
    "github.com/sample/myapp/storage"
)

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    // :typecast
    // :stringer
    // :map Created.UnixMilli() Created
    DomainToStorage(*domain.User) *storage.User
}

Convergen generates optimized conversion functions:

// Code generated by github.com/reedom/convergen
// DO NOT EDIT.

package sample

import (
    "time"
    "github.com/sample/myapp/domain"
    "github.com/sample/myapp/storage"
)

func DomainToStorage(src *domain.User) (dst *storage.User) {
    dst = &storage.User{}
    dst.ID = int64(src.ID)
    dst.Name = src.Name
    dst.Status = src.Status.String()
    dst.Created = src.Created.UnixMilli()
    return
}

docs.title: 🎯 Powerful Annotations
docs.text:
Comprehensive annotation system for fine-grained control over conversions:

  • Field matching: :match name|none - Control how fields are matched
  • Type conversions: :typecast, :stringer - Handle type differences elegantly
  • Custom mappings: :map src dst - Map fields with different names
  • Custom converters: :conv func src dst - Use custom conversion functions
  • Flexible styles: :style return|arg - Control function signatures

docs.title: 🚀 High Performance
docs.text:
• New in v8: 40-70% faster parsing with concurrent processing
  • Smart strategy selection - automatically chooses optimal parsing approach
  • Enterprise reliability with circuit breaker pattern and error recovery
  • Zero runtime dependencies - pure generated code
  • Production ready with extensive testing and performance metrics

docs.title: 🔧 Developer Experience
docs.text:
• Two usage modes: CLI tool or go:generate integration
  • Outputs to .gen.go files by default
  • Comprehensive error messages with suggestions
  • Rich debugging capabilities with behavior-driven testing
  • Extensive test coverage (67%+) and comprehensive documentation

docs.title: 🌟 Advanced Features
docs.text:
• Generics support - Full Go generics compatibility
  • Error handling - Built-in error propagation patterns
  • Preprocessing/Postprocessing - Custom logic injection
  • Complex mappings - Nested struct handling
  • Framework integration - Works with popular Go frameworks

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Choose your preferred installation method:

go:generate (Recommended)CLI ToolGo Module

Add to your Go file: 

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3

Then run: 

go generate ./...

Install globally: 

go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

Use directly: 

convergen your-file.go

By default, generates: your-file.gen.go

Add to your project: 

go get -u github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

docs.title: Domain-to-Storage Conversion
docs.text:
Convert between domain models and storage representations:

type Convergen interface {
    // :typecast
    UserToStorage(*domain.User) *storage.User
    StorageToUser(*storage.User) *domain.User
}

docs.title: API Response Mapping
docs.text:
Transform internal models to API responses:

type Convergen interface {
    // :skip Password
    // :map CreatedAt.Format("2006-01-02") CreatedDate
    UserToResponse(*model.User) *api.UserResponse
}

docs.title: Microservice Communication
docs.text:
Convert between service boundaries:

type Convergen interface {
    // :conv proto.Marshal Data
    ModelToProto(*internal.Model) (*pb.Model, error)
}

docs.text: Problem Without Convergen With Convergen Boilerplate Code Hundreds of manual field assignments Generated automatically Type Safety Runtime errors from missed fields Compile-time validation Maintenance Update multiple conversion functions Single annotation change Performance Generic reflection-based solutions Optimized static code Testing Test every conversion function Test annotations once

docs.title: CLI Commands
docs.text: Complete reference for Convergen's command-line interface, including all options, flags, and usage patterns.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install Convergen as a global CLI tool:

go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

Verify installation:

convergen -version

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
convergen [flags] <input-file>

Example:

# Generate code from converter.go
convergen converter.go

# Output will be written to converter.gen.go

docs.title: -out <path>
docs.text:
Specify the output file path.

Default: <input-file>.gen.go

Examples:

# Custom output filename
convergen -out generated_converters.go converter.go

# Output to different directory
convergen -out ./generated/converters.go converter.go

# Output to stdout (use with -print)
convergen -out /dev/stdout converter.go

docs.title: -dry
docs.text:
Perform a dry run without writing any files.

Use cases: - Preview generated code - Validate syntax and annotations - Debug conversion logic - CI/CD validation

Examples:

# Preview what would be generated
convergen -dry converter.go

# Combine with -print to see output
convergen -dry -print converter.go

docs.title: -print
docs.text:
Print the generated code to stdout in addition to writing to file.

Use cases: - Debugging generated output - Piping to other tools - Logging generation results

Examples:

# Print to console and write to file
convergen -print converter.go

# Print only (with -dry)
convergen -dry -print converter.go

# Pipe to less for paging
convergen -dry -print converter.go | less

docs.title: -log
docs.text:
Enable detailed logging during code generation.

Log information includes: - File parsing progress - Interface discovery - Annotation processing - Field matching decisions - Error details and suggestions

Examples:

# Enable logging
convergen -log converter.go

# Log output goes to <output-file>.log
# E.g., converter.gen.go.log

# View log in real-time
convergen -log converter.go && tail -f converter.gen.go.log

docs.title: -version
docs.text:
Display version information and exit.

Output includes: - Convergen version - Go version used for building - Build date and commit hash

Example:

convergen -version
# Output: convergen v8.0.3 (built with go1.21.5)

docs.title: -help
docs.text:
Display help information and exit.

Example:

convergen -help

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
Convergen uses standard exit codes to indicate success or failure:

Exit Code Meaning Description 0 Success Code generated successfully 1 General Error Invalid arguments, file not found, etc. 2 Parse Error Syntax errors in input file 3 Generation Error Errors during code generation 4 Write Error Cannot write output file

Example usage in scripts:

#!/bin/bash
convergen converter.go
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Code generation successful"
    go build ./...
else
    echo "Code generation failed"
    exit 1
fi

docs.title: Input File Requirements
docs.text:
• Extension: Must be a .go file
  • Syntax: Valid Go syntax
  • Interfaces: Must contain Convergen interfaces
  • Imports: All referenced types must be importable

docs.title: Output File Behavior
docs.text:
• Default naming: <input>.gen.go
  • Overwrite: Existing files are overwritten
  • Permissions: Inherits permissions from input file
  • Build tags: Includes generation metadata

Generated file header:

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# generate.sh - CI script

set -e

echo "Generating code..."
for file in $(find . -name "*_converter.go"); do
    echo "Processing $file"
    convergen -log "$file"

    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "Generation failed for $file"
        exit 1
    fi
done

echo "Verifying no uncommitted changes..."
git diff --exit-code '*.gen.go'

echo "Testing compilation..."
go build ./...

echo "Running tests..."
go test ./...

echo "Code generation completed successfully"

docs.title: Makefile Integration
docs.text:
.PHONY: generate clean test build

# File patterns
CONVERTER_FILES := $(shell find . -name "*_converter.go")
GENERATED_FILES := $(CONVERTER_FILES:.go=.gen.go)

generate: $(GENERATED_FILES)

%.gen.go: %.go
    convergen -log $<

clean:
    find . -name "*.gen.go" -delete
    find . -name "*.gen.go.log" -delete

test: generate
    go test ./...

build: generate
    go build ./...

# Force regeneration
regen:
    $(MAKE) clean
    $(MAKE) generate

docs.title: Common Error Patterns
docs.text:
Interface Not FoundType Resolution ErrorAnnotation Syntax Error

Error: no converter interfaces found in converter.go

Causes: - Interface not named "Convergen" - Missing :convergen annotation for custom names - Interface not exported (lowercase name)

Solutions: 

# Check interface names
grep -n "type.*interface" converter.go

# Look for :convergen annotations
grep -n ":convergen" converter.go

Error: cannot resolve type "User" in converter.go:15

Causes: - Missing import statements - Unexported types - Package path issues

Solutions: 

# Check imports
head -20 converter.go | grep import

# Verify type accessibility
go doc github.com/myorg/myproject/domain.User

Error: invalid annotation syntax ":map ID" at converter.go:12

Causes: - Incorrect annotation format - Missing annotation parameters - Typos in annotation names

Solutions: 

# Show line with error
sed -n '12p' converter.go

# Check annotation syntax
grep -n ":" converter.go

name: Generate and Test

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  generate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4

    - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
      with:
        go-version: '1.21'

    - name: Install Convergen
      run: go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

    - name: Generate code
      run: |
        find . -name "*_converter.go" -exec convergen -log {} \;

    - name: Verify no changes

    - name: Test

docs.title: Build Scripts
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# build.sh - Complete build pipeline

set -euo pipefail

# Configuration
CONVERGEN_VERSION="v8.0.3"
LOG_LEVEL="INFO"

# Functions
log() {
    echo "[$(date +'%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1"
}

install_convergen() {
    log "Installing Convergen $CONVERGEN_VERSION"
    go install "github.com/reedom/convergen@$CONVERGEN_VERSION"
}

generate_code() {
    log "Generating conversion code"

    local files
    files=$(find . -name "*_converter.go" -type f)

    if [ -z "$files" ]; then
        log "No converter files found"
        return 0
    fi

    local count=0
    while IFS= read -r file; do
        log "Processing $file"
        convergen -log "$file"
        ((count++))
    done <<< "$files"

    log "Generated code for $count files"
}

verify_build() {
    log "Verifying build"
    go mod tidy
    go build ./...
    go test ./...
}

main() {
    log "Starting build process"

    # Install tools
    install_convergen

    # Generate code
    generate_code

    # Verify everything works
    verify_build

    log "Build completed successfully"
}

# Run main function
main "$@"

docs.title: go:generate Integration
docs.text: Complete guide for using Convergen with Go's built-in code generation system.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The go:generate integration is the recommended approach for most Go projects. It provides:

  • ✅ Version pinning for reproducible builds
  • ✅ Standard Go tooling integration
  • ✅ Team consistency - same version for everyone
  • ✅ CI/CD friendly - works out of the box

docs.title: 1. Add go:generate Directive
docs.text:
//go:build convergen

package mypackage

import (
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/domain"
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/storage"
)

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    DomainToStorage(*domain.User) *storage.User
}

docs.title: 2. Generate Code
docs.text:
# Generate for current package
go generate

# Generate for all packages
go generate ./...

# Generate for specific packages
go generate ./internal/converters/...

docs.title: Recommended Versioning

docs.title: Domain-Driven Design
docs.text:
Transform between domain models and persistence layers:

type Convergen interface {
    // Domain to persistence
    // :typecast
    // :map ID.String() ID
    // :map CreatedAt.Unix() Created
    UserToPersistence(*domain.User) *persistence.User

    // Persistence to domain
    // :conv uuid.Parse ID ID
    // :conv time.Unix Created CreatedAt
    PersistenceToUser(*persistence.User) (*domain.User, error)
}

docs.title: API Response Patterns
docs.text:
Clean API transformations with security considerations:

type APIConvergen interface {
    // Public API response
    // :skip Password
    // :skip InternalNotes
    // :map CreatedAt.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05Z07:00") CreatedAt
    UserToPublicAPI(*internal.User) *api.PublicUser

    // Admin API with full details
    // :map CreatedAt.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05Z07:00") CreatedAt
    // :map UpdatedAt.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05Z07:00") UpdatedAt
    UserToAdminAPI(*internal.User) *api.AdminUser
}

}

docs.title: Unit Testing Patterns
docs.text:
func TestUserConversions(t *testing.T) {
    user := &domain.User{
        ID:        uuid.New(),
        Email:     "test@example.com",
        CreatedAt: time.Now(),
    }

    // Test domain to API conversion
    apiUser := UserToAPI(user)
    assert.Equal(t, user.Email, apiUser.Email)
    assert.NotEmpty(t, apiUser.CreatedAt)

    // Test storage conversion
    storageUser := UserToStorage(user)
    assert.Equal(t, user.ID.String(), storageUser.ID)
    assert.Equal(t, user.CreatedAt.Unix(), storageUser.Created)
}

docs.title: Integration Testing
docs.text:
func TestAPIIntegration(t *testing.T) {
    // Create test data
    user := createTestUser()

    // Test full pipeline
    apiReq := &api.CreateUserRequest{Email: user.Email}
    model := RequestToModel(apiReq)
    stored := ModelToStorage(model)
    retrieved := StorageToModel(stored)
    response := ModelToResponse(retrieved)

    // Verify round-trip integrity
    assert.Equal(t, user.Email, response.Email)
}

type OptimizedConvergen interface {
    // :literal Items make([]*api.Item, 0, len(src.Items))

    OrderToAPI(*model.Order) *api.Order
}

func convertItems(items []*model.Item) []*api.Item {
    result := make([]*api.Item, len(items))
    for i, item := range items {
        result[i] = ItemToAPI(item)
    }
    return result
}

docs.title: Basic Examples
docs.text: This guide demonstrates common Convergen usage patterns through practical examples. Each example builds on the previous ones, introducing new concepts progressively.

docs.title: Example 1: Simple Field Matching
docs.text:
Scenario: Convert between identical structures

// Source type
type User struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
    Email string
}

// Destination type  
type UserDTO struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
    Email string
}

// Converter interface
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    UserToDTO(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code:

func UserToDTO(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Name = src.Name
    dst.Email = src.Email
    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Automatic matching by field name and type - ✅ Zero configuration needed for identical fields - ✅ Type-safe compilation validation

docs.title: Example 2: Skipping Sensitive Fields
docs.text:
Scenario: Exclude sensitive data from API responses

type User struct {
    ID       int
    Name     string
    Email    string
    Password string    // Sensitive - exclude from API
    APIKey   string    // Sensitive - exclude from API
}

type PublicUserDTO struct {
    ID    int
    Name  string
    Email string
}

type Convergen interface {
    // Skip sensitive fields
    // :skip Password
    // :skip APIKey
    UserToPublic(*User) *PublicUserDTO
}

Generated code:

func UserToPublic(src *User) (dst *PublicUserDTO) {
    dst = &PublicUserDTO{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Name = src.Name
    dst.Email = src.Email
    // Password and APIKey are skipped
    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Security by design - sensitive fields explicitly excluded - ✅ Multiple skip rules supported - ✅ Compile-time validation prevents accidental exposure

docs.title: Example 3: Field Name Mapping
docs.text:
Scenario: Convert between fields with different names

type User struct {
    ID        int
    FirstName string
    LastName  string
    Email     string
}

type UserResponse struct {
    UserID    int    // Different name
    FirstName string // Direct mapping
    LastName  string // Direct mapping
    Email     string
}

type Convergen interface {
    // Map different field names
    // :map ID UserID
    UserToResponse(*User) *UserResponse
}

Generated code:

func UserToResponse(src *User) (dst *UserResponse) {
    dst = &UserResponse{}
    dst.UserID = src.ID
    dst.FirstName = src.FirstName
    dst.LastName = src.LastName
    dst.Email = src.Email
    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Flexible field mapping with :map annotation - ✅ Direct field name mapping for different field names - ✅ Explicit control over field assignments

docs.title: Example 4: Type Conversion
docs.text:
Scenario: Handle type differences between source and destination

type User struct {
    ID     int        // int type
    Age    int        // int type
    Status UserStatus // Custom enum type
}

type UserStatus int
const (
    StatusInactive UserStatus = iota
    StatusActive
    StatusSuspended
)

func (s UserStatus) String() string {
    switch s {
    case StatusActive: return "active"
    case StatusSuspended: return "suspended"
    default: return "inactive"
    }
}

type UserDTO struct {
    ID     int64  // Different numeric type
    Age    string // String representation
    Status string // String representation
}

type Convergen interface {
    // Enable type casting and string conversion
    // :typecast
    // :stringer
    // :conv strconv.Itoa Age Age
    UserToDTO(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code:

func UserToDTO(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = int64(src.ID)              // Type cast applied
    dst.Age = strconv.Itoa(src.Age)     // Custom converter
    dst.Status = src.Status.String()    // String() method used
    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Automatic type casting with :typecast - ✅ String conversion with :stringer - ✅ Custom converters for complex transformations

docs.title: Example 5: Error Handling
docs.text:
Scenario: Handle conversions that can fail

import (
    "errors"
    "strconv"
    "strings"
)

type User struct {
    ID    int
    Email string
    Age   string // String that needs parsing
}

type UserEntity struct {
    ID    int
    Email string
    Age   int    // Parsed integer
}

type Convergen interface {
    // Use converters that can return errors
    // :conv validateEmail Email
    // :conv parseAge Age
    UserToEntity(*User) (*UserEntity, error)
}

func validateEmail(email string) (string, error) {
    if !strings.Contains(email, "@") {
        return "", errors.New("invalid email format")
    }
    return email, nil
}

func parseAge(ageStr string) (int, error) {
    age, err := strconv.Atoi(ageStr)
    if err != nil {
        return 0, errors.New("invalid age format")
    }
    if age < 0 || age > 150 {
        return 0, errors.New("age out of valid range")
    }
    return age, nil
}

Generated code:

func UserToEntity(src *User) (dst *UserEntity, err error) {
    dst = &UserEntity{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Email, err = validateEmail(src.Email)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }
    dst.Age, err = parseAge(src.Age)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }
    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Error propagation handled automatically - ✅ Early return on first error - ✅ Custom validation logic in converter functions

docs.title: Example 6: Receiver Methods
docs.text:
Scenario: Generate methods on existing types

package domain

type User struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
    Email string
}

// Storage types in different package
import "myapp/storage"

type Convergen interface {
    // Generate as receiver method on User
    // :recv u
    ToStorage(*User) *storage.User

    // Also works with return style
    // :recv u
    // :style arg
    FromStorage(*User, *storage.User)
}

Generated code:

// Receiver method generated on User type
func (u *User) ToStorage() (dst *storage.User) {
    dst = &storage.User{}
    dst.ID = int64(u.ID)
    dst.Name = u.Name
    dst.Email = u.Email
    return
}

func (u *User) FromStorage(src *storage.User) {
    u.ID = int(src.ID)
    u.Name = src.Name
    u.Email = src.Email
}

Key Points: - ✅ Domain model enhancement with receiver methods - ✅ Method-style API for clean usage - ✅ Package boundaries handled correctly

docs.title: Example 7: Complex Mappings
docs.text:
Scenario: Combine multiple advanced features

import (
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

type User struct {
    ID        int
    FirstName string
    LastName  string
    Email     string
    Password  string
    CreatedAt time.Time
    Status    UserStatus
    Metadata  map[string]interface{}
}

type UserAPIResponse struct {
    ID          int                    `json:"id"`
    FullName    string                `json:"full_name"`
    Email       string                `json:"email"`
    Status      string                `json:"status"`
    CreatedDate string                `json:"created_date"`
    Version     string                `json:"version"`
    Metadata    map[string]string     `json:"metadata"`
}

type Convergen interface {
    // Complex conversion with multiple features
    // :typecast
    // :stringer
    // :skip Password
    // :conv combineNames FirstName FullName     # Custom converter for name combination
    // :conv formatDate CreatedAt CreatedDate    # Custom converter for date formatting
    // :literal Version "1.0"
    // :conv stringifyMetadata Metadata
    // :preprocess validateUser
    // :postprocess logConversion
    UserToAPI(*User) (*UserAPIResponse, error)
}

func stringifyMetadata(meta map[string]interface{}) map[string]string {
    result := make(map[string]string)
    for k, v := range meta {
        result[k] = fmt.Sprintf("%v", v)
    }
    return result
}

func validateUser(dst *UserAPIResponse, src *User) error {
    if src.Email == "" {
        return errors.New("email is required")
    }
    return nil
}

func logConversion(dst *UserAPIResponse, src *User) error {
    fmt.Printf("Converted user %d to API response\n", src.ID)
    return nil
}

Generated code:

func UserToAPI(src *User) (dst *UserAPIResponse, err error) {
    dst = &UserAPIResponse{}

    // Preprocess hook
    err = validateUser(dst, src)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }

    // Field assignments
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.FullName = combineNames(src.FirstName)
    dst.Email = src.Email
    dst.Status = src.Status.String()
    dst.CreatedDate = formatDate(src.CreatedAt)
    dst.Version = "1.0"
    dst.Metadata = stringifyMetadata(src.Metadata)
    // Password is skipped

    // Postprocess hook
    err = logConversion(dst, src)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }

    return
}

Key Points: - ✅ Multiple features working together - ✅ Pre/post processing hooks for validation and logging - ✅ Complex transformations with custom converters

docs.title: Example 8: Multiple Interfaces
docs.text:
Scenario: Organize converters by domain or purpose

//go:build convergen

package converters

// User-related conversions
// :convergen
type UserConvergen interface {
    // :skip Password
    // :stringer
    UserToPublic(*domain.User) *api.PublicUser

    // :typecast
    UserToStorage(*domain.User) *storage.User
}

// Order-related conversions  
// :convergen
type OrderConvergen interface {
    // :typecast
    // :conv calculateTotal Items Total
    OrderToAPI(*domain.Order) *api.Order

    // :recv o
    ToStorage(*domain.Order) *storage.Order
}

func calculateTotal(items []*domain.OrderItem) decimal.Decimal {
    var total decimal.Decimal
    for _, item := range items {
        total = total.Add(item.Price.Mul(decimal.NewFromInt(int64(item.Quantity))))
    }
    return total
}

Generated code creates multiple functions:

// User conversions
func UserToPublic(src *domain.User) (dst *api.PublicUser) { ... }
func UserToStorage(src *domain.User) (dst *storage.User) { ... }

// Order conversions
func OrderToAPI(src *domain.Order) (dst *api.Order) { ... }
func (o *domain.Order) ToStorage() (dst *storage.Order) { ... }

Key Points: - ✅ Domain organization with multiple interfaces - ✅ Clean separation of concerns - ✅ Flexible naming with :convergen annotation

docs.title: Testing Your Examples
docs.text:
Create test functions to verify your conversions:

func TestUserConversions(t *testing.T) {
    user := &User{
        ID:        1,
        FirstName: "John",
        LastName:  "Doe",
        Email:     "john@example.com",
        Status:    StatusActive,
        CreatedAt: time.Now(),
    }

    // Test public conversion
    publicUser := UserToPublic(user)
    assert.Equal(t, user.ID, publicUser.ID)
    assert.Equal(t, user.Email, publicUser.Email)
    // Password should not be set
    assert.Empty(t, publicUser.Password)

    // Test API conversion
    apiResponse, err := UserToAPI(user)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    assert.Equal(t, "John Doe", apiResponse.FullName)
    assert.Equal(t, "active", apiResponse.Status)
    assert.Equal(t, "1.0", apiResponse.Version)
}

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Convergen can be used in multiple ways to fit different development workflows. Choose the method that best fits your project setup.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.21 or later (required)
  • Go modules enabled (recommended)
  • Git (for go:generate usage)

docs.title: Method 1: go:generate Integration (Recommended)
docs.text:
This is the most common and recommended approach for Go projects.

Add to your Go file:

//go:build convergen

package mypackage

import (
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/internal/domain"
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/internal/storage"
)

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    DomainToStorage(*domain.User) *storage.User
}

Generate code:

go generate ./...

Advantages: - ✅ No global installation required - ✅ Version pinning for reproducible builds - ✅ Integrates with standard Go tooling - ✅ Team-friendly (same version for everyone) - ✅ Works in CI/CD out of the box

docs.title: Method 2: CLI Tool Installation
docs.text:
Install Convergen as a global command-line tool.

Install globally:

go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

Use directly:

# Generate from specific file
convergen input.go

# Custom output location
convergen -out generated.go input.go

# Preview without writing files
convergen -dry input.go

# Enable verbose logging
convergen -log input.go

Advantages: - ✅ Direct command-line usage - ✅ Scriptable for build systems - ✅ No go:generate setup required - ✅ Works with any text editor

CLI Options:

Flag Description Default -out <path> Output file path <input>.gen.go -dry Preview without writing files false -print Also print output to stdout false -log Enable detailed logging false

docs.title: Method 3: Go Module Dependency
docs.text:
Add Convergen as a project dependency for programmatic usage.

Add to your project:

go get github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

Use in custom tooling:

package main

import (
    "context"
    "log"

    "github.com/reedom/convergen/pkg/parser"
)

func main() {
    parser, err := parser.NewParser("input.go", "output.go")
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatal(err)
    }

    result, err := parser.Parse(context.Background())
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatal(err)
    }

    // Use result for custom processing
}

Advantages: - ✅ Programmatic API access - ✅ Custom tooling integration - ✅ Advanced configuration options - ✅ Enterprise workflow integration

docs.title: Recommended Versioning Strategy
docs.text:
For production use, always pin to a specific version:

// ✅ Recommended: Pin to specific version
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3

// ⚠️ Development only: Use latest
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

// ✅ Alternative: Pin to major version (gets patch updates)
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8

docs.title: Version Compatibility
docs.text: Convergen Version Go Version Key Features v8.x Go 1.21+ Concurrent parsing, enhanced performance v7.x Go 1.18+ Basic generics support v6.x Go 1.16+ Legacy compatibility

docs.title: Checking Your Version
docs.text:
# For CLI installation
convergen -version

# For go:generate usage
go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3 -version

# Check installed version
go list -m github.com/reedom/convergen

docs.title: File Organization
docs.text:
A typical project structure:

myproject/
├── go.mod
├── go.sum
├── internal/
│   ├── domain/
│   │   └── user.go
│   ├── storage/
│   │   └── user.go
│   └── converters/
│       ├── converter.go          # Your interface definitions
│       └── converter.gen.go      # Generated code (auto-created)
└── cmd/
    └── myapp/
        └── main.go

docs.title: Build Tags
docs.text:
Use build tags to exclude converter definitions from regular builds:

//go:build convergen

package converters

// This file is only included during code generation
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    // Your conversion methods
}

docs.title: Makefile Integration
docs.text:
.PHONY: generate clean build

generate:
    go generate ./...

clean:
    find . -name "*.gen.go" -delete

build: generate
    go build ./...

test: generate
    go test ./...

docs.title: Test Your Installation
docs.text:
Create a simple test file to verify everything works:

Create test_converter.go:

//go:build convergen

package main

type User struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
}

type UserDTO struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
}

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    UserToDTO(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generate and test:

# Generate code
go generate ./...

# Verify generated file exists
ls -la test_converter.gen.go

# Test compilation
go build -o /dev/null ./...

Expected output:

// Code generated by github.com/reedom/convergen
// DO NOT EDIT.

package main

func UserToDTO(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Name = src.Name
    return
}

docs.title: Common Installation Issues
docs.text:
Go Version Too OldModule Not FoundPermission Denied

Error: go: go.mod requires go >= 1.21

Solution: 

# Check your Go version
go version

# Update Go from https://golang.org/dl/
# Or use version manager like g or gvm

Error: cannot find module providing package

Solution: 

# Ensure Go modules are enabled
go mod init myproject
go mod tidy

# Try the command again
go generate ./...

Error: permission denied: cannot write file

Solution: 

# Check file permissions
ls -la *.gen.go

# Remove readonly generated files
chmod +w *.gen.go

# Or clean and regenerate
find . -name "*.gen.go" -delete
go generate ./...

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running with Convergen in 5 minutes! This guide walks you through creating your first conversion function from scratch.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In this tutorial, you'll:

  1. Set up a simple Go project
  2. Define source and destination types
  3. Create a conversion interface with annotations
  4. Generate the conversion function
  5. Use the generated code in your application

docs.title: Step 1: Project Setup
docs.text:
Create a new Go project:

mkdir convergen-tutorial
cd convergen-tutorial
go mod init github.com/yourusername/convergen-tutorial

docs.title: Step 2: Define Your Types
docs.text:
Let's create a common scenario: converting between domain models and API responses.

Create types.go:

package main

import "time"

// Domain model - your internal representation
type User struct {
    ID        int
    FirstName string
    LastName  string
    Email     string
    Password  string // Sensitive field we don't want in API
    CreatedAt time.Time
    Status    UserStatus
}

type UserStatus int

const (
    StatusInactive UserStatus = iota
    StatusActive
    StatusSuspended
)

func (s UserStatus) String() string {
    switch s {
        case StatusActive:
            return "active"
        case StatusSuspended:
            return "suspended"
        default:
            return "inactive"
    }
}

// API response - what clients receive
type UserResponse struct {
    ID          int    `json:"id"`
    FirstName   string `json:"first_name"`
    LastName    string `json:"last_name"`
    Email       string `json:"email"`
    Status      string `json:"status"`
    CreatedDate string `json:"created_date"`
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create Converter Interface
docs.text:
Create converter.go:

//go:build convergen

package main

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    // Convert User to UserResponse
    // :skip Password                           # Don't copy sensitive field
    // :stringer                                # Use String() method for Status
    // :conv formatTimestamp CreatedAt CreatedDate # Use converter for timestamp formatting
    UserToResponse(*User) *UserResponse
}

docs.title: Step 4: Generate Code
docs.text:
Run the code generator:

go generate ./...

This creates converter.gen.go with your conversion function:

// Code generated by github.com/reedom/convergen
// DO NOT EDIT.

package main

func UserToResponse(src *User) (dst *UserResponse) {
    dst = &UserResponse{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.FirstName = src.FirstName
    dst.LastName = src.LastName
    dst.Email = src.Email
    dst.Status = src.Status.String()
    dst.CreatedDate = formatTimestamp(src.CreatedAt)
    return
}

// Custom converter function (you need to define this)
func formatTimestamp(t time.Time) string {
    return t.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05Z07:00")
}

docs.title: Step 5: Use the Generated Code
docs.text:
Create main.go:

package main

import (
    "encoding/json"
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

func main() {
    // Create a sample user
    user := &User{
        ID:        1,
        FirstName: "John",
        LastName:  "Doe",
        Email:     "john.doe@example.com",
        Password:  "secret123", // This won't appear in the API response
        CreatedAt: time.Now(),
        Status:    StatusActive,
    }

    // Convert to API response using generated function
    response := UserToResponse(user)

    // Serialize to JSON
    jsonData, err := json.MarshalIndent(response, "", "  ")
    if err != nil {
        panic(err)
    }

    fmt.Println("User converted to API response:")
    fmt.Println(string(jsonData))
}

docs.title: Step 6: Run and Test
docs.text:
go run .

Output: 

User converted to API response:
{
  "id": 1,
  "first_name": "John",
  "last_name": "Doe",
  "email": "john.doe@example.com",
  "status": "active",
  "created_date": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z07:00"
}

Notice how: - ✅ Password field was skipped (security) - ✅ Names mapped directly (FirstName, LastName) - ✅ Status enum was converted to string - ✅ Timestamp was formatted for JSON - ✅ No boilerplate code was written manually

docs.title: 1. Annotations Explained
docs.text: Annotation Purpose Result :skip Password Exclude sensitive field Password not copied :stringer Use String() method Status becomes "active" :conv formatTimestamp CreatedAt CreatedDate Custom converter for timestamp ISO 8601 timestamp

docs.title: 2. Generated Function Features
docs.text:
• Type-safe: Compile-time validation of conversions
  • Efficient: Direct field assignments, no reflection
  • Maintainable: Update annotations, regenerate code
  • Readable: Clean, understandable generated code

docs.title: 3. Build Integration
docs.text: The //go:build convergen tag ensures: - Converter definitions don't interfere with regular builds - Clean separation between generation and runtime code - Standard Go tooling integration

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
For conversions that can fail:

type Convergen interface {
    // :conv validateEmail Email
    UserToResponse(*User) (*UserResponse, error)
}

func validateEmail(email string) (string, error) {
    if !strings.Contains(email, "@") {
        return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid email: %s", email)
    }
    return email, nil
}

docs.title: Reverse Conversion
docs.text:
Convert back from API to domain:

type Convergen interface {
    // API to domain conversion
    // :literal Password ""                    # Set empty password
    // :literal CreatedAt time.Now()           # Set current time
    // :conv parseStatus Status                # Parse status string
    ResponseToUser(*UserResponse) *User
}

func parseStatus(status string) UserStatus {
    switch status {
    case "active":
        return StatusActive
    case "suspended":
        return StatusSuspended
    default:
        return StatusInactive
    }
}

docs.title: Multiple Interfaces
docs.text:
Organize by purpose:

// :convergen
type APIConvergen interface {
    UserToResponse(*User) *UserResponse
    ResponseToUser(*UserResponse) *User
}

// :convergen  
type StorageConvergen interface {
    UserToEntity(*User) *storage.UserEntity
    EntityToUser(*storage.UserEntity) *User
}

docs.title: Generation Fails?
docs.text:
# Check Go version (1.21+ required)
go version

# Verify syntax
go build -o /dev/null ./...

# Clean and regenerate
rm -f *.gen.go
go generate ./...

docs.title: Code Won't Compile?
docs.text:
• Check imports: Ensure all referenced types are accessible
  • Verify annotations: Review syntax against annotations reference
  • Validate functions: Ensure custom converter functions exist

docs.title: Unexpected Results?
docs.text:
• Enable logging: Use convergen -log -print converter.go to debug
  • Check generated code: Review *.gen.go files to understand output
  • Test incrementally: Start simple, add complexity gradually

docs.title: Common Annotation Patterns
docs.text:
type Convergen interface {
    // Basic field matching
    // :match name
    Convert(*Source) *Destination

    // With type conversion
    // :typecast
    // :stringer  
    ConvertWithTypes(*Source) *Destination

    // Custom field mapping
    // :map SourceField DestField
    // :conv CustomConverter Field
    ConvertCustom(*Source) *Destination

    // Error handling
    // :conv ValidateAndConvert Field
    ConvertWithError(*Source) (*Destination, error)
}

docs.title: Interface-Level vs Method-Level Annotations
docs.text:
• Interface-level: Apply to all methods in the interface
  • Method-level: Override interface defaults for specific methods
// Interface-level: affects all methods
// :typecast
// :case:off
type Convergen interface {
    // Method-level: overrides for this method only
    // :case
    // :match none
    SpecialConvert(*Source) *Destination

    // Uses interface defaults
    RegularConvert(*Source) *Destination
}

docs.title: Annotations Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all Convergen annotations. This guide covers syntax, usage patterns, and examples for every annotation type.

docs.title: Quick Reference Table
docs.text: Annotation Location Purpose Example :convergen interface Mark interface as converter // :convergen :match <algorithm> interface, method Set field matching strategy :match name :match none :style <style> interface, method Function signature style :style return :style arg :recv <var> method Generate receiver method :recv u :reverse method Reverse copy direction :reverse :case / :case:off interface, method Case sensitivity control :case :case:off :getter / :getter:off interface, method Include getter methods :getter :getter:off :stringer / :stringer:off interface, method Use String() methods :stringer :stringer:off :typecast / :typecast:off interface, method Allow type casting :typecast :typecast:off :skip <pattern> method Skip destination fields :skip Password :skip /^internal/ :map <src> <dst> method Explicit field mapping :map ID UserID :map Name() FullName :conv <func> <src> [dst] method Custom converter function :conv encrypt Email :literal <dst> <value> method Assign literal value :literal CreatedAt time.Now() :preprocess <func> method Pre-conversion hook :preprocess validate :postprocess <func> method Post-conversion hook :postprocess cleanup

docs.title: Interface-Level Annotations
docs.text: These annotations apply to the entire interface and affect all methods unless overridden.

docs.title: :convergen
docs.text:
Marks an interface as a converter definition, allowing interfaces with names other than "Convergen".

Location: Interface Format: // :convergen

Example:

// :convergen
type UserConvergen interface {
    // :recv u
    ToStorage(*User) *storage.User
}

// :convergen
type OrderConvergen interface {
    // :recv o
    ToAPI(*Order) *api.Order
}

Use Cases: - Multiple converter interfaces in one package - Domain-specific naming conventions - Avoiding name conflicts in large projects

docs.title: :match <algorithm>
docs.text:
Sets the field matching strategy for the interface.

Location: Interface, Method Default: :match name Format: :match name | :match none

Algorithms:

namenone

Automatic field matching by name and type

// :match name
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code matches fields automatically: 

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = src.ID      // Matched by name
    dst.Name = src.Name  // Matched by name
    return
}

Only explicit mappings via :map and :conv

// :match none
type Convergen interface {
    // :map ID UserID
    // :map Name FullName
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code only includes explicit mappings: 

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.UserID = src.ID     // Explicit mapping
    dst.FullName = src.Name // Explicit mapping
    return
}

docs.title: :style <style>
docs.text:
Controls the generated function signature style.

Location: Interface, Method Default: :style return Format: :style return | :style arg

Styles:

returnarg

Destination as return value

// :style return
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
    ConvertWithError(*User) (*UserDTO, error)
}

Generated functions: 

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    // ... field assignments
    return
}

func ConvertWithError(src *User) (dst *UserDTO, err error) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    // ... field assignments with error handling
    return
}

Destination as parameter

// :style arg
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*UserDTO, *User)
    ConvertWithError(*UserDTO, *User) error
}

Generated functions: 

func Convert(dst *UserDTO, src *User) {
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Name = src.Name
    // ... other assignments
}

func ConvertWithError(dst *UserDTO, src *User) (err error) {
    dst.ID = src.ID
    // ... assignments with error handling
    return
}

docs.title: Case Sensitivity Control
docs.text:
Controls case-sensitive matching for field names and patterns.

Location: Interface, Method Default: :case (case-sensitive) Format: :case | :case:off

Example:

// Interface-level: case-insensitive by default
// :case:off
type Convergen interface {
    // Method-level: override to case-sensitive
    // :case
    StrictConvert(*User) *UserDTO

    // Uses interface default (case-insensitive)
    FlexibleConvert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Effects: - :case:off allows UserID to match userid, USERID, etc. - Applies to :match name, :getter, and :skip patterns - Does not affect :map and :conv (always case-sensitive)

docs.title: Getter Method Inclusion
docs.text:
Controls whether getter methods are included in field matching.

Location: Interface, Method Default: :getter:off Format: :getter | :getter:off

Example:

type User struct {
    name string // private field
}

func (u *User) Name() string {
    return u.name
}

type UserDTO struct {
    Name string
}

// :getter
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.Name = src.Name() // Uses getter method
    return
}

docs.title: Type Conversion Helpers
docs.text:
Enable automatic type conversions and string formatting.

Location: Interface, Method Default: :stringer:off, :typecast:off Format: :stringer | :stringer:off, :typecast | :typecast:off

Example:

type User struct {
    ID     int
    Status UserStatus // Custom type with String() method
}

type UserStatus int
func (s UserStatus) String() string { /* ... */ }

type UserDTO struct {
    ID     int64  // Different numeric type
    Status string // String representation
}

// :typecast
// :stringer
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = int64(src.ID)        // Type cast applied
    dst.Status = src.Status.String() // String() method used
    return
}

docs.title: Method-Level Annotations
docs.text: These annotations apply to specific methods and override interface-level defaults.

docs.title: :recv <var>
docs.text:
Generates the function as a method with a receiver.

Location: Method Format: :recv <variable>

Requirements: - Receiver type must be in the same package as generated code - Variable name should match existing receiver conventions

Example:

package domain

// User type defined in same package
type User struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
}

type Convergen interface {
    // :recv u
    ToStorage(*User) *storage.User
}

Generated code:

func (u *User) ToStorage() (dst *storage.User) {
    dst = &storage.User{}
    dst.ID = int64(u.ID)
    dst.Name = u.Name
    return
}

docs.title: :reverse
docs.text:
Reverses the copy direction in receiver methods.

Location: Method Requirements: Must use :style arg Format: :reverse

Example:

type Convergen interface {
    // :style arg
    // :recv u
    // :reverse
    FromStorage(*User, *storage.User)
}

Generated code:

func (u *User) FromStorage(src *storage.User) {
    u.ID = int(src.ID)    // Copy FROM src TO receiver
    u.Name = src.Name
}

docs.title: :skip <pattern>
docs.text:
Skips copying to specific destination fields.

Location: Method Format: :skip <field-path> | :skip /<regex>/

Field Path Syntax: - Simple field: :skip Name - Nested field: :skip Address.Street - Regex pattern: :skip /^Internal/

Examples:

type Convergen interface {
    // Skip single field
    // :skip Password
    UserToPublic(*User) *PublicUser

    // Skip multiple fields
    // :skip Password
    // :skip InternalNotes
    // :skip CreatedBy
    UserToAPI(*User) *APIUser

    // Skip with regex
    // :skip /^internal/
    // :skip /Secret$/
    UserToExternal(*User) *ExternalUser
}

docs.title: :map <src> <dst>
docs.text:
Explicitly maps source to destination fields.

Location: Method Format: :map <source-expression> <destination-field>

Source Expression Types:

Field PathMethod CallMethod with Arguments (Use :conv instead)Template Arguments 
// :map ID UserID
// :map Address.Street StreetAddress
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// :map Name() FullName
// :map Status.String() StatusText
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// For complex expressions, use :conv annotation instead:
// :conv formatFullName FirstName FullName  
// :conv formatDate CreatedAt CreatedDate
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

func formatFullName(firstName string) string {
    return firstName + " " + lastName // You'll need both fields
}

func formatDate(createdAt time.Time) string {
    return createdAt.Format("2006-01-02")
}

// :map $1 ExtraField    # First additional parameter
// :map $2 AnotherField  # Second additional parameter
Convert(*User, string, int) *UserDTO

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User, arg0 string, arg1 int) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.UserID = src.ID
    dst.StreetAddress = src.Address.Street
    dst.FullName = src.Name()
    dst.StatusText = src.Status.String()
    dst.ExtraField = arg0
    dst.AnotherField = arg1
    return
}

docs.title: :conv <func> <src> [dst]
docs.text:
Applies custom converter function to field transformation.

Location: Method Format: :conv <function> <source-field> [destination-field]

Converter Function Requirements:

Simple ConverterError-Returning Converter

Signature: func(srcType) dstType

// :conv hashPassword Password
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

func hashPassword(password string) string {
    // Hash the password
    return hashedPassword
}

Signature: func(srcType) (dstType, error)

// :conv validateEmail Email
Convert(*User) (*UserDTO, error)

func validateEmail(email string) (string, error) {
    if !isValidEmail(email) {
        return "", errors.New("invalid email")
    }
    return email, nil
}

Examples:

import (
    "crypto/sha256"
    "encoding/hex"
    "errors"
    "strings"
)

type Convergen interface {
    // :conv hashPassword Password
    // :conv validateEmail Email  
    // :conv formatName Name FullName
    Convert(*User) (*UserDTO, error)
}

func hashPassword(password string) string {
    hash := sha256.Sum256([]byte(password))
    return hex.EncodeToString(hash[:])
}

func validateEmail(email string) (string, error) {
    if !strings.Contains(email, "@") {
        return "", errors.New("invalid email format")
    }
    return strings.ToLower(email), nil
}

func formatName(name string) string {
    return strings.Title(strings.ToLower(name))
}

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO, err error) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.Password = hashPassword(src.Password)
    dst.Email, err = validateEmail(src.Email)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }
    dst.FullName = formatName(src.Name)
    return
}

docs.title: :literal <dst> <value>
docs.text:
Assigns literal values to destination fields.

Location: Method Format: :literal <destination-field> <expression>

Example:

import "time"

type Convergen interface {
    // :literal CreatedAt time.Now()
    // :literal Version "1.0"
    // :literal IsActive true
    // :literal Count len(src.Items)
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User) (dst *UserDTO) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Name = src.Name
    dst.CreatedAt = time.Now()
    dst.Version = "1.0"
    dst.IsActive = true
    dst.Count = len(src.Items)
    return
}

docs.title: Processing Hooks
docs.text:
Execute custom functions before or after the main conversion logic.

Location: Method Format: :preprocess <function> | :postprocess <function>

Function Signatures:

Without ErrorWith ErrorWith Additional Arguments 
func preprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType) {}
func postprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType) *DstType {}

func preprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType) error {}
func postprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType) error {}

func preprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType, arg0 int, arg1 string) error {}
func postprocess(dst *DstType, src *SrcType, arg0 int, arg1 string) error {}

Example:

type Convergen interface {
    // :preprocess validateInput
    // :postprocess auditConversion
    Convert(*User, string) (*UserDTO, error)
}

func validateInput(dst *UserDTO, src *User, context string) error {
    if src.Email == "" {
        return errors.New("email is required")
    }
    return nil
}

func auditConversion(dst *UserDTO, src *User, context string) error {
    log.Printf("Converted user %d in context %s", src.ID, context)
    return nil
}

Generated code:

func Convert(src *User, arg0 string) (dst *UserDTO, err error) {
    dst = &UserDTO{}

    // Preprocess hook
    err = validateInput(dst, src, arg0)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }

    // Main conversion logic
    dst.ID = src.ID
    dst.Email = src.Email
    // ... other field assignments

    // Postprocess hook
    err = auditConversion(dst, src, arg0)
    if err != nil {
        return
    }

    return
}

docs.title: Combining Annotations
docs.text:
Multiple annotations work together to create sophisticated conversion logic:

type Convergen interface {
    // Complex conversion with multiple annotations
    // :typecast                                    # Enable type casting
    // :stringer                                   # Use String() methods
    // :skip Password                              # Skip sensitive data
    // :skip /^internal/                           # Skip internal fields
    // :map ID UserID    # Field mapping
    // :conv hashEmail Email HashedEmail           # Custom conversion
    // :literal CreatedAt time.Now()               # Set creation time
    // :preprocess validateUser                    # Validate before conversion
    // :postprocess logConversion                  # Log after conversion
    ComplexConvert(*User) (*UserDTO, error)
}

docs.title: Conditional Logic
docs.text:
Use custom converters for conditional logic:

// :conv setStatus Status
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

func setStatus(user *User) string {
    if user.IsActive && user.EmailVerified {
        return "active"
    } else if user.IsActive {
        return "pending"
    }
    return "inactive"
}

docs.title: Collection Handling
docs.text:
Handle slices and maps with custom converters:

// :conv convertTags Tags
// :conv convertMetadata Metadata
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

func convertTags(tags []string) []string {
    result := make([]string, len(tags))
    for i, tag := range tags {
        result[i] = strings.ToLower(tag)
    }
    return result
}

func convertMetadata(meta map[string]interface{}) map[string]string {
    result := make(map[string]string)
    for k, v := range meta {
        result[k] = fmt.Sprintf("%v", v)
    }
    return result
}

docs.title: Annotation Organization
docs.text:
type Convergen interface {
    // Group related annotations together
    // Field matching configuration
    // :match name
    // :case:off
    // :getter
    //
    // Type conversion helpers  
    // :typecast
    // :stringer
    //
    // Field-specific rules
    // :skip Password
    // :map ID UserID
    // :conv hashEmail Email
    //
    // Processing hooks
    // :preprocess validate
    // :postprocess audit
    Convert(*User) (*UserDTO, error)
}

docs.title: Error Handling Patterns
docs.text:
type Convergen interface {
    // Simple conversion (no errors expected)
    // :typecast
    SimpleConvert(*User) *UserDTO

    // Validation conversion (errors possible)
    // :conv validateEmail Email
    // :conv checkAge Age
    ValidatedConvert(*User) (*UserDTO, error)

    // Complex conversion with hooks
    // :preprocess authorize
    // :conv encryptData Data
    // :postprocess auditLog
    SecureConvert(*User, *AuthContext) (*UserDTO, error)
}

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
type Convergen interface {
    // Pre-allocate slices for better performance
    // :literal Items make([]*Item, 0, len(src.Items))
    // :conv convertItems Items
    OptimizedConvert(*Order) *OrderDTO
}

func convertItems(items []*OrderItem) []*ItemDTO {
    // Efficient slice conversion
    result := make([]*ItemDTO, len(items))
    for i, item := range items {
        result[i] = ItemToDTO(item)
    }
    return result
}

docs.title: Self-Diagnosis Checklist
docs.text: Before seeking help, run through this checklist:

docs.title: ✅ Environment Check
docs.text:
• Go version 1.21 or later installed
  • Convergen properly installed or accessible
  • Project uses Go modules (go.mod exists)
  • Working directory is correct

docs.title: ✅ Code Structure Check
docs.text:
• Interface properly defined and exported
  • All referenced types are imported and accessible
  • Annotation syntax follows documented format
  • Custom converter functions exist and are accessible

docs.title: ✅ Generation Process Check
docs.text:
• go:generate directive is correct
  • File permissions allow reading/writing
  • No conflicting generated files exist
  • Build tags are properly configured

docs.title: ✅ Output Validation Check
docs.text:
• Generated code file exists and is readable
  • Generated code compiles without errors
  • Generated functions have expected signatures
  • Runtime behavior matches expectations

docs.title: Information to Provide
docs.text:
When reporting issues, include:

  1. Environment Details 

    go version
# Output: go version go1.21.5 darwin/amd64

  2. Convergen Version 

    go run github.com/reedom/convergen@latest -version
# Or check go.mod for version

  3. Minimal Reproduction Case 

    // Complete minimal example that reproduces the issue
//go:build convergen
package example

//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3
type Convergen interface {
    // Your problematic interface definition
}

  4. Error Messages 

    # Complete error output including stack traces

  5. Expected vs Actual Behavior

  6. What you expected to happen
  7. What actually happened
  8. Any workarounds you've tried

docs.text:
Problem: cannot find module providing package github.com/reedom/convergen

Solutions:

docs.title: Interface Not Found
docs.text:
Problem: no converter interfaces found in converter.go

Common Causes & Solutions:

Wrong Interface NameUnexported InterfaceMissing Build Tag

Issue: Interface not named "Convergen"

Solution: 

// ❌ Wrong - not recognized
type MyConverter interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

// ✅ Correct - auto-recognized
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

// ✅ Alternative - use annotation
// :convergen
type MyConverter interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Issue: Interface name starts with lowercase

Solution: 

// ❌ Wrong - unexported
type convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

// ✅ Correct - exported
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

Issue: Interface in file without proper build tag

Solution: 

//go:build convergen

package mypackage

// Now the interface will be found
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

docs.title: Type Resolution Errors
docs.text:
Problem: cannot resolve type "User" in converter.go:15

Diagnostic Steps:

# 1. Check if type is imported
grep -n "import" converter.go

# 2. Verify type accessibility
go doc github.com/myorg/myproject/domain.User

# 3. Check if type is exported
grep -n "type User" domain/*.go

Common Solutions:

Missing ImportUnexported TypeCircular Import 
// ❌ Missing import
type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO  // User not found
}

// ✅ Add import
import "github.com/myorg/myproject/domain"

type Convergen interface {
    Convert(*domain.User) *UserDTO
}

// ❌ In domain package - unexported
type user struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
}

// ✅ Export the type
type User struct {
    ID   int
    Name string
}

Problem: Converter package imports domain, domain imports converter

Solution: Move converters to separate package 

project/
├── domain/
│   └── user.go
├── api/
│   └── user.go
└── converters/        # Separate package
    ├── converter.go
    └── converter.gen.go

docs.title: Annotation Syntax Errors
docs.text:
Problem: invalid annotation syntax at converter.go:12

Common Syntax Issues:

Missing ParametersIncorrect FormatInvalid Field Path 
// ❌ Wrong - missing destination
// :map Name
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// ✅ Correct - include destination  
// :map Name FullName
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// ❌ Wrong - missing colon
// skip Password
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// ✅ Correct - starts with colon
// :skip Password
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// ❌ Wrong - field doesn't exist
// :map NonExistentField Name
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

// ✅ Correct - valid field path
// :map FirstName Name
Convert(*User) *UserDTO

docs.text:
Problem: undefined: SomeType in generated code

Diagnostic Commands:

# Check generated code
cat converter.gen.go

# Test compilation
go build -o /dev/null ./...

# Check for missing imports
go list -e -json ./... | jq '.Error'

Common Solutions:

Missing Imports in Generated CodeType Conversion Issues

Issue: Generated code lacks required imports

Solution: Ensure all types are properly imported in converter file 

// Add all necessary imports
import (
    "time"
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/domain"
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/api"
    "github.com/myorg/myproject/storage"
)

Issue: Incompatible types in assignment

Solution: Use type conversion annotations 

// Enable automatic type casting
// :typecast
type Convergen interface {

docs.title: Custom Converter Function Issues
docs.text:
Problem: undefined: customConverter in generated code

Solutions:

Function Not AccessibleWrong Function Signature 
// ❌ Wrong - unexported function
func customConverter(s string) string {
    return strings.ToUpper(s)
}

// ✅ Correct - export function or define in same file
func CustomConverter(s string) string {
    return strings.ToUpper(s)
}

type Convergen interface {
    // :conv CustomConverter Name
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

// ❌ Wrong - doesn't match expected signature
func CustomConverter() string {
    return "default"
}

// ✅ Correct - matches field type
func CustomConverter(input string) string {
    return strings.ToUpper(input)
}

// ✅ With error handling
func CustomConverter(input string) (string, error) {
    if input == "" {
        return "", errors.New("empty input")
    }
    return strings.ToUpper(input), nil
}

docs.title: Nil Pointer Dereference
docs.text:
Problem: panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference

Solutions:

Nil Source ObjectNil Nested Objects 
// Add nil checks in your code
func Convert(src *User) *UserDTO {
    if src == nil {
        return nil  // or return empty object
    }
    return UserToDTO(src)  // Generated function
}

// Use safe navigation in custom converters
func SafeGetAddress(user *User) string {
    if user == nil || user.Address == nil {
        return ""
    }
    return user.Address.Street
}

type Convergen interface {
    // :conv SafeGetAddress Address
    Convert(*User) *UserDTO
}

docs.title: Type Assertion Failures
docs.text:
Problem: panic: interface conversion: interface {} is nil, not string

Solution: Add type safety in custom converters

func SafeStringConvert(v interface{}) string {
    if v == nil {
        return ""
    }

    switch val := v.(type) {
    case string:
        return val
    case fmt.Stringer:
        return val.String()
    default:
        return fmt.Sprintf("%v", val)
    }
}

docs.title: Slow Generation
docs.text:
Problem: Code generation takes too long

Solutions:

Update to v8Optimize Project StructureUse Concurrent Parser (v8) 
# Update to latest version for performance improvements
go get github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

# Avoid deep package hierarchies
# Minimize cross-package dependencies
# Use specific imports instead of wildcard imports

Automatic in v8, but you can configure: 

// For programmatic usage
config := parser.NewConcurrentParserConfig()
modernParser := parser.NewModernParser(config)

find . -name "*.gen.go" -delete

docs.title: Docker Build Issues
docs.text:
Problem: Generation fails in Docker container

Solution:

# Multi-stage build
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder

# Install Convergen
RUN go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest

WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download

COPY . .

# Generate code
RUN find . -name "*_converter.go" -exec convergen {} \;

# Build app
RUN go build -o app .

# Final stage
FROM alpine:latest
RUN apk --no-cache add ca-certificates
COPY --from=builder /app/app /usr/local/bin/
ENTRYPOINT ["app"]

**Input file:**

Actual behavior: What actually happened

Error output: 

Complete error message and stack trace

Additional context: Any other relevant information ```

docs.title: Migration Guide
docs.text: Guide for upgrading between Convergen versions, including breaking changes and compatibility information.

docs.title: Performance Improvements
docs.text:
v8 introduces significant performance improvements:

  • 40-70% faster parsing with concurrent processing
  • Smart strategy selection
  • Enterprise-grade reliability features

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
v8 is fully backward compatible with v7 code:

  • All existing annotations work unchanged
  • Generated code maintains same API
  • No breaking changes to interface definitions

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: • Go 1.21+ (updated from Go 1.18+ in v7)

docs.title: Upgrade Steps
docs.text:
1. Update Go version to 1.21+
  2. Update Convergen version: 
    # For go:generate
//go:generate go run github.com/reedom/convergen@v8.0.3

# For CLI
go install github.com/reedom/convergen@latest
  3. Regenerate code: 
    go generate ./...
  4. Test compilation: 
    go build ./...
go test ./...

• Concurrent parser engine
  • Enhanced error handling
